questions.explanation: Why C: Multiple valid architectural options with different infrastructure implications = plan mode + team approval. WebSockets (bi-directional, persistent connections), SSE (simpler, one-directional), Polling (simplest infra). Trade-offs affect deployment, costs, scaling — team must decide. A: Deliberate technical debt. B: Assumes answer without evaluating. D: Delegates architectural decisions to Claude — humans must own architecture and infrastructure choices.

questions.title: Slash Commands – Team Workflow
questions.text: Your team wants Claude to follow a detailed code review checklist (8 items) when reviewing pull requests — invoked when starting reviews. Claude is also used extensively for writing features, debugging, and generating documentation. Currently, developers manually paste the checklist at the start of each review session. Which approach best addresses this workflow need?

options.A: Create a `/review` slash command containing the checklist, invoked when starting reviews
options.B: Create a dedicated review subagent with the checklist embedded in its configuration
options.C: Configure plan mode as the default for code review sessions
options.D: Add the checklist to the project's CLAUDE.md file under a 'Code Review' section

questions.explanation: Why A: /review slash command = on-demand invocation. Developer types /review → checklist loaded → review begins. Doesn't appear for feature writing, debugging, or documentation tasks. Eliminates manual pasting. Team uses it consistently. B is over-engineering for a slash command job. C: Plan mode is unrelated to executing a checklist. D: CLAUDE.md loads for EVERY session — the checklist would pollute feature writing, debugging, and documentation sessions with irrelevant review content. End of Study Guide All 60 questions covered. You're ready for the exam.

questions.title: Behavioral Guidelines Persistence
questions.text: Where should you define behavioral guidelines (enthusiastic tone, explain reasoning, ask clarifying questions) to persist reliably across all user interactions?

options.A: In the first assistant message, instructing Claude to follow these guidelines going forward
options.B: In the system prompt
options.C: In environmental variables that your application passes to the API client
options.D: Prepended to each user message

questions.explanation:
Why B: The system prompt is specifically designed for persistent behavioral instructions that apply to every
interaction. Environmental variables have no effect on model behavior.

questions.title: Contradictory User Preferences
questions.text: User stated "very low risk tolerance" but later says "I want to maximize returns." Asks "What should I invest in?" Which approach best ensures the recommendation aligns with actual priority?

options.A: Proceed with the most recently stated preference
options.B: Provide separate recommendations for both scenarios without clarifying which the user prefers
options.C: Recommend a balanced portfolio without explicitly addressing the conflict
options.D: Surface the contradiction and ask the user to clarify which matters more—capital preservation or growth potential—before recommending

questions.explanation:
Why D: When preferences directly contradict each other, the correct approach is to surface the conflict and ask
for clarification rather than silently picking one or splitting the difference.

questions.title: Conversation Memory Not Working
questions.text: Claude asks what genres a user enjoys even though they said "I love jazz" two messages earlier. Most likely cause?

options.A: Claude requires a vector database connection to maintain conversation memory
options.B: Your application isn't including prior messages in the messages array
options.C: The Claude API requires a session_id parameter that you haven't configured
options.D: The model's context window has been exceeded by the conversation length

questions.explanation:
Why B: Claude has no inherent memory. The application must include all prior conversation turns in the
messages array with each API request.

questions.title: System Prompt Update Breaks Existing Conversations
questions.text: Updated system prompt improves quality for new users but causes contradictions and style changes for long-running multi-session users. Best approach?

options.A: Add instructions to the new prompt directing the assistant to maintain consistency with prior statements
options.B: Version system prompts and associate each conversation with the prompt version under which it started, applying updates only to new conversations
options.C: Add a transition message when sessions resume explaining behavior may differ
options.D: Regenerate summaries of existing conversations using the new prompt

questions.explanation:
Why B: Versioning prompts per conversation preserves consistency for existing users while allowing
improvements for new ones. Regenerating histories (D) risks altering the meaning of past exchanges.

questions.title: Implicit Expertise Signals
questions.text: Fitness assistant defaults to overly detailed responses when users don't explicitly state experience level, despite correct behavior for explicit declarations. Which system prompt change addresses this?

options.A: Implement a pre-conversation intake asking users to rate their experience level
options.B: Replace most conditionals with a general principle: "Adapt explanation depth to match user expertise, mirroring their terminology." Keep only the safety-critical conditional about injury consultations
options.C: Add more conditional branches for additional expertise signals
options.D: Add an explicit instruction for the model to ask a clarifying question about experience level when unclear

questions.explanation:
Why B: A general principle allows the model to apply judgment to implicit cues (like technical terminology). More
conditionals (C) fail on cases not explicitly covered.

questions.title: Long Conversation Context Management (Book Club, 85K tokens)
questions.text: Users ask about conclusions from earlier sessions but get generic responses. Discussions build on previous conclusions. Most effective approach?

options.A: Implement rolling window truncation to keep only the most recent 25,000 tokens
options.B: Use semantic embedding to index the full conversation history and retrieve only relevant past exchanges
options.C: Implement progressive summarization where older conversation blocks are replaced with concise summaries that explicitly extract key conclusions, decisions, and recurring themes, while keeping recent exchanges verbatim
options.D: Add structured XML tags to mark significant discussion conclusions throughout the conversation history

questions.explanation:
Why C: Progressive summarization explicitly preserves key conclusions (the valuable signal) while freeing up
context space. Simple truncation (A) loses the conclusions.

questions.title: 40% Abandonment Rate from Too Many Clarifying Questions
questions.text: Assistant asks 3 clarifying questions at once; 40% of users abandon. Best approach to reduce friction while handling ambiguity?

options.A: Create a lookup table of common request patterns with predefined default interpretations
options.B: Add a preprocessing step using a smaller model to classify request ambiguity on a 1–5 scale
options.C: Limit the assistant to one clarifying question per tur
options.D: Modify the system prompt to instruct the assistant to make reasonable assumptions from available context, state those assumptions explicitly, and offer to adjust if the interpretation is wrong

questions.explanation:
Why D: Making transparent assumptions and inviting correction reduces friction dramatically while still producing
useful responses. Over-engineering (B) adds latency and complexity.

questions.title: Story Writing: Context Limits, Persistent vs. Ephemeral Content
questions.text: After 40+ turns story assistant "forgets" character traits. Persistent elements (world rules, character backgrounds) vs. ephemeral brainstorming. Best approach?

options.A: Apply a sliding-window keeping only the most recent 25 turns
options.B: Summarize the entire conversation history every 20 turns
options.C: Separate persistent story elements into a retained "story bible" section at context start, applying trimming or summarization only to brainstorming discussion
options.D: Store all history in a vector database and retrieve semantically similar passages

questions.explanation:
Why C: This directly addresses the two-category problem. The story bible stays in context permanently; only
ephemeral brainstorming is compressed.

questions.title: Research Assistant Loses Precision After 60K Tokens
questions.text: Precision-dependent questions (exact p-values, sample sizes) become hedged/inaccurate after summarization. Most effective architectural change?

options.A: Implement retrieval that re-injects relevant paper sections when questions suggest numerical details are needed
options.B: Maintain a structured database of key facts extracted from each paper (sample sizes, statistics, methods) and retrieve relevant entries into context when precision-dependent questions are detected
options.C: Use a separate Claude call with explicit instructions to generate higher-fidelity summaries
options.D: Keep source text from methodology and results sections permanently in context

questions.explanation:
Why B: A structured fact database directly solves the precision problem—exact numbers are stored and
retrieved, bypassing the lossy summarization step.

questions.title: Real Estate Assistant Ignores Updated Preferences
questions.text: Users update budget/property type mid-conversation but assistant references original preferences. Context at only 35%. Most reliable solution?

options.A: Include few-shot examples showing the assistant correctly acknowledging and applying preference changes
options.B: Add system prompt instructions emphasizing most recently stated preferences over earlier ones
options.C: Maintain a structured state object with current preferences, update it on changes, and include it i each request
options.D: Implement conversation pruning to remove turns containing outdated preferences

questions.explanation:
Why C: A managed state object explicitly tracks the current value. This is more reliable than hoping the model
notices updates in a long conversation (B) or pruning history (D).

questions.title: Event Planning: 4.2 Average Clarifying Questions, 35% Abandonment
questions.text: Reducing questions causes poor recommendations; keeping them causes abandonment. Best trade-off?

options.A: Configure the assistant to consolidate all clarifying questions into a single compound questio
options.B: Instruct the assistant to state explicit assumptions based on conversation history, proceed with recommendations while inviting corrections, and reserve clarifying questions only for irreversible actions like confirming bookings
options.C: Implement a structured intake form that collects all required parameters upfront
options.D: Configure the assistant to proceed with reasonable defaults without explicitly stating these assumptions

questions.explanation:
Why B: This threads the needle perfectly—reduce questions while remaining transparent and correctable.
Reserving questions for irreversible actions is the right risk-calibrated approach.

questions.title: Ambiguous First Message: "Set up my focus music"
questions.text: Could mean configure preferences, create playlist, or play immediately. Best approach?

options.A: Create a new "Focus" playlist with curated tracks and notify the user it's ready
options.B: Start preference configuration by asking about genres, tempo, and artists
options.C: Ask one clarifying question about action type: play now or configure for later
options.D: Play popular focus tracks immediately and let the user redirect if needed

questions.explanation:
Why C: The fundamental ambiguity is between immediate playback and setup—one targeted question resolves
this cleanly without launching into a lengthy preference flow.

questions.title: 78K Token Dinner Planning: Critical Safety Info + General Chat
questions.text: Session contains: shellfish allergy, recipe measurements, user-defined terms, and general back-and-forth. Best context management?

options.A: Implement a sliding window retaining only the most recent 20,000 tokens
options.B: Extract critical structured data (allergies, serving counts, user-defined terms) into a compact reference section, summarize general discussion, and retain recent exchanges verbatim
options.C: Store the full conversation externally and use semantic search
options.D: Summarize the entire conversation history into a concise summary

questions.explanation:
Why B: The allergies and user-defined terms must never be lost—they're safety-critical. This approach treats
different content types differently, which is the right architecture.

questions.title: Latency and Costs Rise After 50+ Conversation Turns
questions.text: PRIMARY cause of increasing API latency and costs?

options.A: The model generates progressively longer responses as it accumulates more context
options.B: The entire conversation history is included with each API request, so input tokens grow with every turn
options.C: The model builds an internal profile of the user's conversation patterns
options.D: Database operations for retrieving and storing conversation history slow dow

questions.explanation:
Why B: Claude is stateless. Every request re-sends the full conversation history. As turns accumulate, input
tokens grow linearly, directly increasing both cost and latency.

questions.title: Repetitive Response Openers ("Certainly!", "I'd be happy to help!")
questions.text: Users report responses feel repetitive across turns. Most effective approach?

options.A: Append a partial assistant message with a direct response opening that the model will continue from
options.B: Implement post-processing to detect and strip common greeting phrases from response beginnings
options.C: Lower the temperature parameter
options.D: Add system prompt instructions specifying phrases to avoid, such as "Never begin responses with 'Certainly' or similar affirmations"

questions.explanation:
Why D: System prompt instructions address the root cause. Post-processing (B) is brittle and adds latency;
lowering temperature (C) doesn't control phrasing choices.

questions.title: Tool Ordering: extract_metadata Must Run Before Enrichment Tools
questions.text: Claude sometimes calls lookup_citations before extract_metadata. Most effective fix?

options.A: Set tool_choice to "any" combined with system prompt instructions
options.B: Set tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"} and process the enrichment requests in subsequent turns after receiving the extracted metadata
options.C: Set tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"} for every API call in the pipeline
options.D: Set tool_choice to "auto" and reorder tool definitions

questions.explanation:
Why B: Option C forces extract_metadata on EVERY call, meaning enrichment tools can never run. Option B
correctly sequences: force extraction in turn 1, then allow enrichment in subsequent turns.

questions.title: property_type Enum Failing for Unusual Types (studio, loft, duplex)
questions.text: 8% of extractions fail schema validation. New property types keep appearing. Best long-term solution?

options.A: Continuously expand the enum to include newly observed property types
options.B: Add few-shot examples demonstrating how to map unexpected types to the closest existing enum value
options.C: Add an "other" value to your enum with a separate property_type_detail string field for specifics when "other" is selected
options.D: Change property_type from an enum to a free-form string and implement normalization in post-processing

questions.explanation:
Why C: This maintains the structure and queryability of an enum while gracefully accommodating any future
edge case through the detail field.

questions.title: Standard Reports (Batch OK) vs. Urgent Exception Reports (30-min SLA)
questions.text: Minimize costs while meeting latency requirements. Architecture?

options.A: Submit all documents to the Batch API with custom_ids for tracking
options.B: Route standard reports to the Batch API for 50% cost savings, and route urgent exception reports to the real-time Messages API
options.C: Queue all documents and submit hourly batches
options.D: Submit all documents to the real-time Messages API

questions.explanation:
Why B: The correct architecture matches the tool to the requirement. Batch = cost savings for non-urgent.
Real-time = required for the 30-min SLA.

questions.title: Batch Processing Strategy: 30-Hour SLA, Batch API Has 24-Hour Window
questions.text: Documents arrive continuously. Which batching frequency satisfies 99.9% reliability within 30 hours?

options.A: Submit a single batch at end of day
options.B: Submit batches every 6 hours (6 + 24 = 30 hrs — no buffer)
options.C: Use the real-time API for all documents instead of batch processing
options.D: Submit batches every 4 hours containing documents from that window

questions.explanation:
Why D: Max wait in queue = 4 hrs. Max processing = 24 hrs. Total = 28 hrs, leaving a 2-hour buffer within the
30-hour SLA while still getting the 50% batch discount.

questions.title: Inconsistent Skill Extraction (skills: string[])
questions.text: Issues: compound phrases sometimes split/kept, implied skills appear, wildly different array lengths. Current prompt: "Extract all skills mentioned." Most effective improvement?

options.A: Enrich schema to {skill: string, confidence: float, source_quote: string}[]
options.B: Add few-shot examples demonstrating compound phrase handling, explicit mention criteria, and appropriate entry granularity
options.C: Add post-extraction normalization that maps skills to a canonical taxonomy
options.D: Add constraints: "Extract 10-20 skills maximum, one skill per entry, only explicitly named skills"

questions.explanation:
Why B: All three issues are judgment calls (what counts as one skill, what counts as explicit, what granularity is
appropriate). Few-shot examples demonstrate the exact behavior rather than imposing potentially incorrect rules.

questions.title: Accuracy Drops from 98% to 71% for Long Documents
questions.text: Tool definitions are 2,500 tokens. Documents 150K → 98% accuracy; 175-190K → 71%. Context window is 200K. Most likely cause?

options.A: The model distributes attention proportionally, causing less focus on content near the end
options.B: Schemas exceeding 8-10 fields increase decision complexity
options.C: Tool definitions consume input context tokens. Combined with system prompts and document content, the total approaches the context limit, degrading end-of-document processing
options.D: Very long documents exceed the model's effective attention spa

questions.explanation:
Why C: 2,500 (tool def) + ~2,000 (system prompt) + 175,000-190,000 (document) ≈ 180-195K, approaching the
200K limit. Content near the end of long documents gets cut off or degraded.

questions.title: Model Generates Plausible but Incorrect Values for Missing Fields
questions.text: Event extraction: model outputs "500" for attendee_count when article contains no attendance information. Most effective fix?

options.A: Add a post-processing step using a second LLM call to verify each extracted value exists in the source document
options.B: Upgrade to a more capable model tier
options.C: Add prompt instructions to return null for any field where information is not directly stated in the source
options.D: Make all schema fields required (non-nullable)

questions.explanation:
Why C: This directly addresses the root cause—the model needs explicit permission and instruction to return null
rather than hallucinate plausible values.

questions.title: Invoice Extraction: 18% Mismatch Between Line Items Sum and Grand Total
questions.text: Downstream systems reject mismatched records. Most effective approach?

options.A: Add a "calculated_total" field where the model sums extracted line items alongside a "stated_total" field. Flag records for human review when values differ
options.B: Add few-shot examples demonstrating invoices where extracted line items sum correctly
options.C: Implement post-processing that automatically adjusts line item amounts proportionally when their sum doesn't match the stated total
options.D: Extract line items and totals independently, then use a separate validation model

questions.explanation:
Why A: This captures both values and surfaces discrepancies for human judgment. Auto-adjusting proportionally
(C) silently corrupts data—the discrepancy might be a real OCR error or a real model error that needs human
review.

questions.title: 300/10,000 Documents Failed with "context_length_exceeded"
questions.text: Most cost-effective approach to process only the failures?

options.A: Reprocess the entire batch with prompt caching enabled
options.B: Resubmit only the 300 failed documents after chunking them into smaller pieces, then combine the partial extractions
options.C: Increase the max_tokens parameter for the 300 failed documents and resubmit in a new batch
options.D: Resubmit the entire 10,000 document batch using a model tier with a larger context window

questions.explanation:
Why B: context_length_exceeded means the INPUT is too long—max_tokens (C) controls output length, not
input. Chunking the documents that are too large is the correct fix, and reprocessing only the 300 failures is the
most cost-efficient approach.

questions.title: Retry-with-Error-Feedback: Which Pattern Is LEAST Helped by Retries?
questions.text: For which failure would additional retries be LEAST effective?

options.A: The model extracts "et al." for co-authors when the full list exists only in an external document not in the input
options.B: The model extracts citation counts as locale-formatted strings ("1,234") when the schema requires integers
options.C: The model extracts dates as ISO 8601 datetime strings when the schema requires only the date portio
options.D: The model extracts keywords as a nested object when the schema requires a flat array of strings

questions.explanation:
Why A: B, C, and D are all format/structure errors that can be fixed by error feedback on retry. A is fundamentally
different—the data doesn't exist in the input at all. No amount of retrying can produce information that isn't there.

questions.title: 12% Semantic Validation Failure (Amounts Don't Sum, Invalid Vendor IDs)
questions.text: JSON syntax never fails. Most effective approach to reduce manual review?

options.A: Implement post-processing logic that automatically corrects common errors
options.B: Retry the extraction up to 3 times when validation fails, accepting first result that passes
options.C: When validation fails, make a follow-up request with the document, extraction, and validatio errors for model correction
options.D: Add stricter schema constraints with detailed field descriptions to prevent invalid values initially

questions.explanation:
Why C: Giving the model the full context (document + failed extraction + specific errors) lets it understand exactly
what went wrong and fix it precisely. Stricter constraints (D) help with output format but not semantic validity
(sums, ID formats).

questions.title: Contract Amendments: Model Extracts Original or Amendment Inconsistently
questions.text: "30-day payment terms" vs "45 days" in Amendment 1. Most effective improvement?

options.A: Post-extraction validation using pattern matching to flag amendments for manual review
options.B: Add prompt instructions to always extract the most recent amendment value and ignore superseded original terms
options.C: Redesign the schema so amended fields capture multiple values, each with source location and effective date
options.D: Preprocess documents with a classifier that identifies and removes superseded sections

questions.explanation:
Why C: Legal contracts often need both values preserved with provenance. Discarding original terms (B, D)
loses legally important history. The schema redesign is the most complete and accurate solution.

questions.title: Inconsistent "Materials" Field Output
questions.text: "cotton blend" vs "Cotton/Polyester mix" vs field omitted when present. Most effective fix?

options.A: Add few-shot examples showing 2-3 complete input-output pairs with standardized material description formats
options.B: Make the "materials" field required instead of optional to force the model to always extract a value
options.C: Switch to a more capable model tier
options.D: Set temperature to 0 to eliminate randomness and ensure deterministic outputs

questions.explanation:
Why A: Temperature 0 reduces variation but doesn't tell the model WHICH format to use. Few-shot examples
directly demonstrate the desired output format.

questions.title: High-Confidence Extractions (≥85%) Still Have 12% Error Rate
questions.text: Errors from comparison tables, appendices, ambiguous phrasing. Need sustainable strategy to catch errors AND measure improvement over time.

options.A: Add a verification pass that re-extracts from each high-confidence document, flagging where two attempts differ
options.B: Lower the confidence threshold from 85% to 70%
options.C: Implement heuristic rules that flag documents containing comparison tables or appendices for review regardless of confidence score
options.D: Implement stratified random sampling reviewing a fixed percentage of high-confidence extractions weekly, enabling error rate measurement and novel pattern detection

questions.explanation:
Why D: This is the only approach that enables ongoing error rate measurement AND discovers novel failure
patterns. Heuristics (C) only catch known patterns.

questions.title: Automating High-Confidence Extractions (97% Aggregate Accuracy)
questions.text: System has 100% human review for 3 months. Before deploying automation, what validation is most critical?

options.A: Analyze accuracy by document type and field to verify high-confidence extractions perform consistently across all segments, not just in aggregate
options.B: Verify that 97% accuracy meets requirements for all downstream systems that consume the extracted data
options.C: Compare accuracy at different confidence thresholds (85%, 90%, 95%) to find the optimal cutoff
options.D: Run a two-week pilot routing 25% of high-confidence extractions directly to downstream systems

questions.explanation:
Why A: 97% aggregate accuracy can hide 70% accuracy on specific document types or fields. Segment-level
analysis before automation prevents deploying a system that silently fails on particular cases.

questions.title: Customer Demands Human Agent NOW (No Investigation Done Yet)
questions.text: Customer says: "I want to talk to a real person NOW." Agent has not called any tools. What should the agent do?

options.A: Immediately call escalate_to_human with the conversation history
options.B: Acknowledge the frustration and ask one targeted question to understand the specific issue before escalating
options.C: First call get_customer and lookup_order to gather account context, then escalate to a human agent (was selected)
options.D: Briefly explain what the agent can help with and offer to resolve the issue quickly

questions.explanation:
Why A: The customer has issued an explicit, emphatic request for a human. Delaying that by gathering context
first (C) adds more friction to an already frustrated customer.

questions.title: Designing Escalation Trigger Logic
questions.text: Which approach most reliably identifies cases that genuinely require human intervention?

options.A: Instruct the agent to escalate when the customer requests a human, when the issue requires policy exceptions, or when the agent cannot make meaningful progress
options.B: Implement sentiment analysis that monitors for frustration indicators and triggers when frustration score exceeds a threshold
options.C: Configure the agent to escalate after three consecutive tool calls that fail to resolve the stated issue
options.D: Build a rules engine that maps specific issue types, customer segments, and product categories to escalation decisions

questions.explanation:
Why A: This covers the three genuine escalation conditions. Rules engines (D) are brittle and miss edge cases;
sentiment analysis (B) can misfire or miss real needs.

questions.title: process_refund Returns Technical Errors (5%) and Business Errors (12%)
questions.text: Agent wastes 3-4 turns retrying permanent business errors. Both return plain text. Most effective fix?

options.A: Implement automatic retry logic at the tool level for technical errors only
options.B: Add a check_refund_eligibility tool that must be called before process_refund
options.C: Return structured error responses with retriable: false for business errors and a customer-friendly explanation for Claude to use
options.D: Add few-shot examples showing how to distinguish retriable from non-retriable errors

questions.explanation:
Why C: Structured error metadata directly tells the agent whether to retry or not. This eliminates wasted turns
without restructuring the entire workflow (B).

questions.title: Frustrated Customer Wants Human, But Return Is Straightforward
questions.text: "I've been going back and forth for days. I just want to speak to someone." Return is within policy and eligible for immediate processing.

options.A: Process the refund via process_refund to resolve the underlying issue, then inform them it's complete
options.B: Call escalate_to_human immediately to honor the customer's request
options.C: Ask what specifically hasn't worked in previous attempts before deciding
options.D: Acknowledge frustration, inform them this is resolvable now, and offer to complete it or escalate

questions.explanation:
Why D: The customer's real goal is resolution. Acknowledging their frustration, showing the problem is solvable,
and giving them the choice respects their experience while delivering maximum value.

questions.title: lookup_order Called Multiple Times; Tool Outputs Dominate Context
questions.text: 40+ fields per response, most of context is tool output. Customer mentions 2 more orders. Most effective before additional lookups?

options.A: Move all tool responses to a vector database with semantic indexing
options.B: Extract only return-relevant fields (items, purchase date, return window, status) from each existing order response, removing verbose details
options.C: Have the model generate a natural language summary of each order's key details, replacing structured responses with prose
options.D: Proceed with additional lookups without modifying the existing tool output context

questions.explanation:
Why B: Extracting only the fields relevant to the current task (returns) preserves the data needed while
dramatically reducing context usage. Natural language summaries (C) lose structured data needed for
subsequent tool calls.

questions.title: Mid-Process Escalation: Billing Dispute Requires Manager Approval
questions.text: Agent has gathered context via get_customer and lookup_order but issue exceeds authorization limit. How to handle the escalation?

options.A: Persist the complete conversation and tool response history to a database, then call escalate_to_huma with a reference ID
options.B: Call escalate_to_human passing only the customer's original message
options.C: Attempt the refund with process_refund anyway, escalating only if the system rejects the transactio
options.D: Compile a structured handoff with customer details, order info, and the identified issue before calling escalate_to_human

questions.explanation:
Why D: A structured, synthesized summary gives the human agent exactly what they need for effective
resolution—more useful than a raw reference ID (A) or just the original message (B).

questions.title: Compliance: Refunds >$500 Must Always Escalate (3% Still Slip Through)
questions.text: Despite clear system prompt instructions, 3% failure rate persists. How to achieve guaranteed compliance?

options.A: Add few-shot examples to the prompt showing correct escalation behavior at various refund amounts
options.B: Strengthen the system prompt with emphatic language: "CRITICAL POLICY: Refunds over $500 MUST trigger human escalation. NEVER process these directly."
options.C: Modify the refund tool to return an error with message "Amount exceeds policy limit—please escalate" when threshold is exceeded
options.D: Implement a hook to intercept tool calls; when the refund process amount exceeds $500, block it and invoke human escalation

questions.explanation:
Why D: If the system prompt already fails 3% of the time, stronger wording (B) won't help. A hook that intercepts
and blocks the tool call at the infrastructure level is the only approach that guarantees enforcement regardless of
model behavior.

questions.title: Agent Asks for Customer's Name Again After They Already Provided It
questions.text: Multi-step identity verification; agent re-asks for name after the third verification question. Most likely cause?

options.A: Claude's memory retention is limited to two conversational turns by default
options.B: The conversation history isn't being passed in subsequent API requests
options.C: The prompt lacks instructions telling Claude to remember information across multiple exchanges
options.D: The verification tool is clearing the agent's internal state after each successful validation step

questions.explanation:
Why B: Claude is stateless. If prior turns aren't included in the messages array, the model has no access to what
was established earlier in the conversation.

questions.title: MCP Tool lookup_order: Correct Pattern for Error Communication
questions.text: Backend sometimes returns "Order not found" or temporary database failures. What is the correct pattern?

options.A: Log the error server-side and return an empty result to avoid confusing the model
options.B: Return a success response with a "status" field indicating the error type
options.C: Return the error message in the tool result content with the isError flag set to true
options.D: Throw an exception from the tool handler so the agent framework can catch and log it

questions.explanation:
Why C: This is the correct MCP protocol for tool errors. The agent receives structured information about the
failure and can decide how to respond appropriately.

questions.title: process_refund Timeout: Agent Has Context but Cannot Execute Refund
questions.text: Agent retrieved customer info and order details but process_refund times out. What approach best balances resolution with error handling?

options.A: Explain the billing, confirm refund eligibility, acknowledge the system issue preventing immediate processing, and offer escalation or retry later
options.B: Implement automatic retries with exponential backoff for process_refund, keeping the conversation ope until the refund is successfully processed
options.C: Confirm the refund will be processed and close the conversatio
options.D: Escalate immediately to a human agent since the refund action cannot be completed

questions.explanation:
Why A: The agent provides maximum value with available information, is transparent about the technical
limitation, and gives the customer actionable next steps. Blocking on retries (B) holds the customer hostage to an
uncertain backend recovery.

questions.title: Escalation Handoff After 25+ Turns: $847 Refund
questions.text: Billing dispute resolved: duplicate charges from payment gateway timeout. Human agent won't have transcript access. What context to pass?

options.A: A structured summary: customer ID, root cause, refund amount, and recommended action
options.B: The customer's original complaint verbatim plus tool result excerpts showing duplicate transactions
options.C: Your diagnosis and the refund amount only
options.D: The complete conversation transcript with all tool results

questions.explanation:
Why A: A synthesized structured summary is what the human agent needs to act quickly. The complete
transcript (D) is too much; only the diagnosis (C) is too little.

questions.title: Customer Raises 3 Issues in One Session (Turns 1–45), Context Approaching
questions.text: Limits At turn 48, customer asks about their refund from turns 1–15. Strategy to maintain all issues?

options.A: Rely on MCP tools to re-fetch relevant information on demand when the customer references earlier issues
options.B: Implement sliding window context that retains the most recent 30 turns
options.C: Extract and persist structured issue data (order IDs, amounts, statuses) into a separate context layer
options.D: Summarize earlier turns into a narrative descriptio

questions.explanation:
Why C: A structured data layer persists the key facts from all three issues independently of the conversation
window, so any issue can be referenced accurately at any point in the session.

questions.title: Customer Returns 4 Hours Later with Stale Tool Data
questions.text: Previous session has lookup_order results with "Status: PENDING, Expected resolution: 24-48 hours." Agent references this stale data even after fresh tool calls return different information. Most reliable approach?

options.A: Resume with full history and add a system prompt instruction telling the agent to always prefer the most recent tool results when multiple calls to the same tool exist
options.B: Resume with full history and configure the agent to automatically re-call all previously-used tools at session start
options.C: Resume with full history but filter out previous tool_result messages before resuming
options.D: Start a new session, inject a structured summary of the previous interaction (issue type, actions taken, resolution status), then make fresh tool calls before engaging

questions.explanation:
Why D: Starting fresh with a summary and fresh data guarantees no stale results can influence responses.
Option A still leaves stale data in context and relies on the model to correctly prioritize the newer results.

questions.title: Inconsistent Error Handling Due to Uniform Error Responses
questions.text: MCP tool returns {"isError": true, "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Operation failed"}]} for all errors. Agent retries incorrectly, escalates prematurely, or asks for clarification inappropriately. Most effective improvement?

options.A: Implement retry logic with exponential backoff in your MCP server for all errors
options.B: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating how to interpret error message patterns
options.C: Enhance error responses with structured metadata: include errorCategory (transient/validation/permission), isRetryable boolean, and a description of what caused the failure
options.D: Create an analyze_error MCP tool the agent calls after any failure

questions.explanation:
Why C: Structured error metadata gives the agent exactly the information it needs to choose the right response
strategy for each type of failure without any ambiguity.

questions.title: How Does the Agentic Loop Choose Next Tool After lookup_order Returns
questions.text: 45-Day-Old Order? After receiving order details showing purchase 45 days ago, how does the agentic loop determine whether to call process_refund or escalate_to_human?

options.A: The orchestration layer automatically routes to the next tool based on the order's status field
options.B: The agent executes the remaining steps in a tool sequence planned at the start of the request
options.C: The agent follows a pre-configured decision tree mapping order attributes to specific tool calls
options.D: The order details are added to the conversation and the model reasons about which action to take

questions.explanation:
Why D: This is how Claude's agentic loops work. Tool results become part of the conversation context as
tool_result messages, and the model uses those results plus the system prompt policy to reason about the
next step.

questions.title: MCP Servers from Three Vendors: readOnlyHint vs. destructiveHint Annotations
questions.text: Team proposes bypassing confirmation for tools annotated readOnlyHint: true. What should guide this decision?

options.A: Implement a verification layer testing each tool's actual behavior before adding it to an allowlist
options.B: MCP servers run as local processes, so tools from properly initialized connections can be trusted regardless of vendor
options.C: Treat tool annotations as untrusted metadata unless the server itself is trusted. Base confirmatio requirements on your assessment of each vendor's trustworthiness, not on their self-reported annotations
options.D: Read-only annotations indicate intended behavior and are reliable for tools that perform GET requests

questions.explanation:
Why C: A server can annotate any destructive tool as read-only. Annotations are self-reported metadata, not
independently verifiable. Trust the vendor, not the annotation.

questions.title: MCP Server vs. Custom Tool for Weather API (Multiple Apps Need Weather Data)
questions.text: Primary advantage of the MCP approach when multiple AI applications need weather data?

options.A: Any MCP-compatible client can connect to your weather server without writing custom integratio code
options.B: MCP servers process tool calls faster than custom implementations because they use an optimized binary protocol
options.C: MCP provides built-in retry logic that automatically handles failed API calls with exponential backoff
options.D: MCP automatically handles authentication and rate limiting with the weather API, reducing backend implementation work

questions.explanation:
Why A: The primary value of MCP is standardized reusability—build the integration once, and any
MCP-compatible client (current or future) can use it. MCP does not automatically handle auth or rate limiting (D).

questions.title: post_content Confirmation: 98% Approved in 2 Seconds, But Incidents Occur
questions.text: Posts went to wrong accounts, scheduled at wrong times, or contained errors—all confirmed by users without catching mistakes. How to redesign?

options.A: Include the complete post text, target account, scheduled time, and platform in the confirmatio request
options.B: Auto-approve routine posts and only require explicit confirmation for unusual patterns like posting to new accounts or large audiences
options.C: Add a mandatory waiting period before the confirm option becomes available
options.D: Require users to type a confirmation phrase instead of clicking a butto

questions.explanation:
Why A: Users were rubber-stamping confirmations because the confirmation didn't show them what they were
approving. Making the full details visible in the confirmation surfaces the errors before they happen.

questions.title: search_documents Tool: Database Selection Parameter Design
questions.text: Three databases; users express naturally ("search the research database"). How to design the parameter?

options.A: A freeform string parameter where the backend uses semantic matching to determine which database(s) to search
options.B: No explicit parameter—search all three databases by default, then have the model filter results by source
options.C: An enum parameter with values ["research_papers", "internal_reports", "technical_specs"], requiring the model to map natural language to the appropriate value
options.D: A freeform string parameter with runtime validation that returns an error if the value doesn't match a know database

questions.explanation:
Why C: Enum parameters are ideal here. The model reliably maps natural language to enum values, and the
parameter prevents invalid values at the schema level.

questions.title: archive_file vs delete_file: Agent Calls Wrong Tool for "Remove Old Backups"
questions.text: Both tools have minimal descriptions. Agent calls delete_file when company policy requires archiving backups. Which change most directly improves tool selection?

options.A: Implement server-side validation that rejects delete_file calls for files tagged as backups
options.B: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating that requests involving "backup" or "old" should use archive_file
options.C: Add a confirmation step that requires users to type "CONFIRM DELETE" before delete_file executes (was selected)
options.D: Expand tool descriptions to clarify use cases, adding guidance like "Do not use for backup files" to delete_file and when to use archive_file

questions.explanation:
Why D: The model makes tool selection decisions based on descriptions. Clear, accurate descriptions with
explicit guidance about use cases directly solve the tool selection problem at the source.

questions.title: get_portfolio_value: Structured JSON vs. Formatted Text String
questions.text: Primary advantage of using structured output with defined fields?

options.A: Structured JSON is processed deterministically by the model, significantly improving accuracy whe extracting values
options.B: Structured JSON consumes significantly fewer tokens than natural language
options.C: The agent can reliably extract specific values without parsing free-form text, reducing errors i subsequent operations
options.D: JSON schemas automatically validate that the underlying API returned correct data before the agent processes it

questions.explanation:
Why C: The primary advantage is reliable, error-free data access in subsequent agent steps. Free-form text
requires error-prone parsing; structured fields are directly accessible.

questions.title: remove_team_member dry_run Parameter Bypassed in 15% of Cases
questions.text: Agent calls with dry_run=false directly, skipping the required preview+confirm workflow. Most reliable enforcement?

options.A: Add server-side validation that permits dry_run=false only when a dry_run=true call with identical parameters occurred within the past 60 seconds
options.B: Add detailed instructions and few-shot examples to the tool descriptio
options.C: Replace with two tools: preview_remove_member returns impact details and a single-use confirmation token; execute_remove_member requires that token, binding execution to the specific previewed action
options.D: Annotate the tool as requiring confirmation and configure the orchestration layer to prompt the user for approval

questions.explanation:
Why C: The single-use confirmation token structurally enforces the preview→confirm sequence. It is
architecturally impossible to skip the preview, regardless of how the agent is prompted.

questions.title: delete_contact: 8% Reversals Due to Misidentified Records (Similar Names)
questions.text: Current multi-step confirmation adds friction. Reduce error rate while maintaining efficiency?

options.A: Present matched records with differentiating fields and require single-click confirmation of the intended target before executing deletion
options.B: Deploy automated duplicate detection that identifies and merges probable duplicates
options.C: Implement soft-delete with a 30-day recovery window
options.D: Require users to supply the exact record ID from the CRM interface rather than using natural language references

questions.explanation:
Why A: This directly addresses the root cause (misidentification from similar names) by surfacing differentiating
information before confirming, while keeping the workflow to a single confirmation click.

questions.title: send_notification Timeouts: Cannot Determine If Message Was Sent
questions.text: Currently returns generic is_error: true, agents retry, causing duplicate notifications. How to modify error response?

options.A: Return is_error: true with a structured field retry_safe: true for timeouts
options.B: Return is_error: false with the original message content echoed back
options.C: Return is_error: true with a message encouraging retry
options.D: Return is_error: true with a message communicating uncertainty: "Timeout—status unknown. Message may have been sent. Avoid retry."

questions.explanation:
Why D: The critical information is the delivery uncertainty and the instruction NOT to retry. Option A with
retry_safe: true would cause the exact duplicate problem you're trying to prevent.

questions.title: send_notification Timeouts: Cannot Determine If Message Was Sent
questions.text: Currently returns generic is_error: true, agents retry, causing duplicate notifications. How to modify error response?

options.A: Return is_error: true with a structured field retry_safe: true for timeouts
options.B: Return is_error: false with the original message content echoed back
options.C: Return is_error: true with a message encouraging retry
options.D: Return is_error: true with a message communicating uncertainty: "Timeout—status unknown. Message may have been sent. Avoid retry."

questions.explanation: Why D: The critical information is the delivery uncertainty and the instruction NOT to retry. Option A with retry_safe: true would cause the exact duplicate problem you're trying to prevent.

questions.title: search_flights Tool: 503 Service Unavailable from External Airline API
questions.text: Most effective way to handle this error in your tool implementation?

options.A: Return an error message in the tool result explaining the service is temporarily unavailable
options.B: Log the error internally and return an empty response, letting the model continue without the flight data
options.C: Return an empty flight list as if the search succeeded but found no matching flights
options.D: Automatically retry the request up to five times with exponential backoff before returning results

questions.explanation:
Why A: Returning a clear error message (with isError: true) lets the agent understand the failure and
communicate it to the user. An empty response (B) is ambiguous. An empty results list (C) misleads the agent.
Auto-retrying 5 times (D) could add tens of seconds of latency to every 503 error.

questions.title: process_reimbursement: $500 Approval Threshold Must Be Tamper-Proof
questions.text: Which design ensures the threshold cannot be bypassed regardless of how the agent is prompted?

options.A: Provide two tools (auto_reimburse hard-coded to $500 and request_manager_approval) with system prompt instructions and audit logging
options.B: process_reimbursement accepts an approved_by_manager boolean; prompt instructs agent to only set this to true after confirming approval
options.C: process_reimbursement internally enforces the threshold: amounts ≤$500 auto-disbursed, amounts >$500 create a pending approval request and return a status indicating manager review is pending
options.D: Implement a PreToolUse hook that inspects the amount parameter and modifies the tool call to add requires_approval: true when threshold is exceeded

questions.explanation:
Why C: The business rule is enforced at the tool implementation level, completely independent of the agent's
behavior. The agent cannot bypass it regardless of how it is prompted.

questions.title: log_workout: 23% Invalid Measurement Combinations
questions.text: Cardio exercises get "reps", strength exercises get "miles". Most effective fix?

options.A: Add enum constraints on measurement limiting values to "minutes", "miles", "reps", or "sets"
options.B: Split into log_cardio_workout (with duration_minutes or distance_miles parameters) and log_strength_workout (with reps and sets parameters)
options.C: Add explicit examples to the tool description showing valid combinations (e.g., "For running: use minutes or miles. For push-ups: use reps")
options.D: Implement server-side validation returning descriptive errors for invalid combinations

questions.explanation:
Why B: Splitting tools by exercise type makes invalid combinations structurally impossible. The correct
parameters are enforced by the schema itself—no documentation, examples, or validation needed.

questions.title: Scheduling Agent: 15% "Slot No Longer Available" Failures (Race Condition)
questions.text: get_available_slots then book_appointment. Another user books in between. How to redesign?

options.A: Modify book_appointment to return failure information including currently available alternative slots
options.B: Combine both tools into a single find_and_book_appointment that atomically checks and books
options.C: Add a hold_slot(provider_id, slot_time) tool that creates a 60-second temporary reservation, requiring the agent to call it between checking availability and booking
options.D: Keep both tools but add retry logic in the system prompt instructing the agent to call get_available_slots again if booking fails

questions.explanation:
Why C: This directly solves the race condition by creating a temporary hold. The workflow becomes:
get_available_slots → hold_slot → show options → book_appointment, with the held slot protected from
concurrent booking.

questions.title: Document Extraction Confidence Scores: Two Problems
questions.text: (1) Agent proceeds with low-confidence extractions that are incorrect 23% of the time. (2) Agent requests unnecessary review for high-confidence extractions that are actually correct 31% of the time. How to restructure tool output?

options.A: Compute an aggregate extraction_quality score across all fields with a text summary
options.B: Return fields with raw confidence scores and add few-shot examples for interpretatio
options.C: Return fields organized into verified and needs_verification objects based on confidence thresholds
options.D: Return fields with confidence scores, plus a requires_review boolean computed using your tested confidence thresholds, along with a review_reasons array explaining which fields triggered review

questions.explanation:
Why D: The requires_review boolean (computed from calibrated, tested thresholds) directly solves both
problems: it stops unnecessary reviews for correctly-extracted high-confidence fields, and it flags genuinely
uncertain low-confidence fields. The review_reasons array helps human reviewers focus their attention.
